claims.text: Chase Hughes's NCI (Narrative Credibility Index) is a 20-question test that scores any piece of communication 0–100 to detect manipulation: scores above 70 are full SCOPs, under 40 are probably legitimate, and 40–70 are SCOPs built around a kernel of truth.

evidence.quote:
NCI
engineered reality scoring system.
Now, this is a 20 question test designed
to detect manipulation. The guy who
built it, Chase Hughes, spent two
decades training military and
intelligence personnel, Navy Seals, CIA,
FBI. This guy literally trains the
people who run SCOPS for a living now. I
followed his work for years and his
system is so simple but so brutally
effective it completely changed the way
I consume news. So I told Sean's team,
"Hey, I'm thinking of covering Chase
Hughes and the NCI test." Perfect. Chase
is on the show this week. I believe in
signs. That was a sign. Now, when
creators say this is the video they
don't want you to see, it's usually
clickbait and I should know because I've
done it. But this truly is information
that the people in power don't want you
to know. This is the secret sauce, the
11 herbs and spices. Today, we're going
to name names. We're going to speak
truth to power. And yes, I'm going to
expose people you admire.

NCI stands for narrative
credibility index. And Chase Hughes
studied hundreds of scops, government
propaganda, corporate lies, social media
manipulation, all of it. And he found 20
patterns that appear consistently in
every SCOP.
Things like timing, emotional
manipulation, tribalism, specific
messaging. 20 questions score each 1
through five. Total above 70, that's a
SCOP. Below 40, probably legit. Between
40 and 70, that's the most dangerous of
all because that's a SCOP based around
the truth, but with an agenda. And that
agenda is never to help you. Okay,
that's the scoring system to keep in
mind. 0 to 100. Here we go. October
10th, 1990.
Now, f

claims.rationale: Chase Hughes is a real behavior analyst who has published frameworks for detecting influence operations and trained military/intelligence personnel; the NCI scoring system as described here is consistent with his published work. The framework itself is heuristic and not externally validated as a forensic tool, but the host is faithfully describing Hughes's system, not inventing it.

claims.text: The 1990 testimony of 'Nayirah,' a 15-year-old Kuwaiti girl who claimed to have witnessed Iraqi soldiers throwing babies out of incubators, was a fabricated PR campaign used to manufacture US public support for the Gulf War.

evidence.quote:
15-year-old Kuwaiti girl named Naria
testified before Congress. She was
volunteering at a hospital when Iraqi
soldiers stormed in.
>> The second week after an invasion, I
volunteer volunteered at the Aladan
Hospital with 12 other women. While I
was there, I saw the Iraqi soldiers come
into the hospital with guns. They took
the babies out of the incubators.
Took the incubators and left the
children to die on the cold floor.
It was horrifying.
I could not help but think of my nephew
who born premature might have died that
day as well. Theis have destroyed
everything in Kuwait. They they stripped
the supermarkets of food, thearmacies of
medicine, the factories of medical
supplies supplies, ransacked their
houses, and tortured neighbors and
friends.

Though we didn't know it at the
time. 10 years later, the truth came
out. Naria was the daughter of the
Kuwaiti ambassador. There were no dead
babies. She was never at the hospital.
>> It was all part of a campaign to turn
Saddam Hussein, at least in the public
uh uh consciousness, into Adolf Hitler.
And the the feeling was that they
couldn't sell the Gulf War without this.
In other words, they had to cheat to
win.
>> The NCI system predicted the lie a
decade before anyone admitted to it.

claims.rationale: The Nayirah testimony hoax is one of the best-documented cases of war PR manipulation. She was Nayirah al-Sabah, daughter of Kuwait's US ambassador Saud Nasir al-Sabah; her testimony was coached by Hill+Knowlton; investigators (e.g., Amnesty's MacArthur, John R. MacArthur's 'Second Front') showed there was no evidence of the incubator atrocities. Host's description matches the public record.

claims.text: PR firm Hill & Knowlton was the operational center of both the Gulf War incubator hoax and the mid-20th-century tobacco industry's organized campaign of cancer-doubt — making it a recurring engine of high-impact corporate/political SCOPs.

evidence.quote:
collapse. We're talking billions of
dollars. Millions of jobs all gone
overnight. So they called in a
specialist,
John W. Hill, co-founder of Hill and
Nolton, the PR firm that would later
orchestrate the lie that dragged us into
the Gulf War. Hill understood that doubt
was more powerful than truth. People
wanted to smoke. They like smoking, but
if it was dangerous, most people would
stop. But if it might be dangerous,
they'd keep smoking. So Hill and Nolton
worked their strategy.

claims.rationale: Hill & Knowlton's role in both campaigns is documented in journalism and academic histories: John W. Hill co-founded the firm and personally directed the 1953 tobacco-industry response that produced the Tobacco Industry Research Committee; the firm later represented Citizens for a Free Kuwait and coached Nayirah. The host's framing 'PR firm that would later orchestrate the lie' is editorial but the underlying facts are well-established.

dependencies.rationale: Identifies the PR firm behind the Nayirah testimony

claims.text: The tobacco industry's strategy was not to argue that smoking was safe but to manufacture scientific doubt — a tactic explicitly captured in the 1969 Brown & Williamson memo 'Doubt is our product' — and that doubt-manufacturing template has since been reused across fossil fuels, sugar, and opioids.

evidence.quote:

product since it is the best means of

exists in the minds of the general
public. Doubt is the product and the

let's score it. Cherrypicked data, five
out of five. They only published studies
that supported their narrative. Studies
that showed cancer risks were buried or
attacked. Authority overload, five out
of five. They recruited credentialed
scientists. They created fake research
co

ed.
The fossil fuel industry used the same
type of manufactured reality to cast
doubt on global warming, which became
climate change, which became
controversial. And that's perfect.
Remember, it doesn't matter if the
climate is changing. What matters is
that people keep fighting about it.
The sugar industry said sugar played no
role in diabetes or heart disease. They
blamed fat instead. Remember when there
was a lot of food labeled low-fat that
was marketed as healthy? Well, wasn't
healthy at all. And you know what? Those
low-fat products were loaded with sugar.
Now, opioids might be the most
documented corporate scop in modern
history. False claims that Oxycontton
wasn't addictive. They paid doctors to
lie. Scientific studies were written by
PR agencies, not by scientists.
>> A source tel

claims.rationale: The 'doubt is our product' memo is real (1969 Brown & Williamson, by Frederick Panzer), and Naomi Oreskes's 'Merchants of Doubt' rigorously documents how the same playbook moved from tobacco to acid rain, ozone, climate change, and (in part) the sugar/fat war and opioid marketing. The opioid-Purdue civil settlements substantiate the company-funded-science claim.

dependencies.rationale: Drills into the specific tobacco campaign that Hill orchestrated

claims.text: Facebook's engagement-maximizing algorithm functions as a SCOP by amplifying ethnic-hate content for ad revenue, with real-world death tolls — including the murder of Ethiopian professor Meareg Amare in 2021 after an inflammatory Facebook post that the platform refused to remove until eight days after his killing.

evidence.quote:
Mayar Amare taught chemistry.
60 years old, father of four. He was
toran living in the Amhara region during
the Ethiopian civil war. That made him a
target. A Facebook page with 50,000
followers posted his photo. They called
him a huta. Accused him of being an
enemy. They posted his home address.
Now, his son reported the post to
Facebook right away multiple times.
Begged them to take the post down. No
response. 3 weeks later, men followed
the professor home from work. They
executed him in front of his house. The
killers chanted Hunter. as they walked
away. The same word from the Facebook
posts. 8 days after the murder, Facebook
finally responded and removed the posts.
8 days too late.
Francis Hogan was a product manager at
Facebook. She quit in May 2021, 4 months
before this murder. She testified before
Congress about what was happening in
Ethiopia. She said the platform's
algorithm was causing ethnic violence.
Posts that made people angry got more
engagement.

claims.rationale: Meareg Amare was killed in November 2021; his son and Amnesty/Foxglove later sued Meta in Kenya, alleging Facebook posts incited the murder and were not removed despite repeated reports. Frances Haugen's leaked Facebook Files documented internal warnings about engagement-driven amplification of hate, including in Ethiopia. Specific causal share of Facebook in 'hundreds of thousands of deaths' is harder to verify quantitatively, so I'm holding directTruth below 0.9.

claims.rationale: The February 19, 2020 Lancet letter is real, was organized by Peter Daszak, and his EcoHealth Alliance funded research at the Wuhan Institute of Virology — facts later confirmed when emails surfaced via FOIA in 2021 and the Lancet eventually appended a competing-interest disclosure (June 2021). Whether this constituted 'a SCOP' is editorial, but the conflict-of-interest factual core is well-substantiated.

claims.text: Both the 'natural origin is settled science' and 'lab leak is obvious' camps in the COVID-19 origin debate scored as SCOPs (70 and 66 respectively), demonstrating that the same manipulation patterns can run on both sides of a debate, denying the public any genuinely honest investigation.

claims.rationale: This is host opinion using his own scoring framework. The descriptive observation that both pro- and anti-lab-leak factions used PR/social-media tactics is defensible and matches public-record behavior on both sides; the precise NCI scores are subjective. I'm withholding directTruth — the framing 'both sides scored as SCOPs' is a reasonable but value-laden claim, not a verifiable fact.

dependencies.rationale: Adds the 'other side also a SCOP' move to the lab-leak case

claims.text: Self-described UFO whistleblower Jake Barber's 2025 News Nation disclosure should be treated skeptically because the rollout exhibits classic SCOP markers: timed for maximum attention, hyperbolic 'changes everything' messaging, undisclosed financial incentive (his Sky Watchers LLC and a forthcoming book), and unverifiable footage.

evidence.quote:
Jake Barber
stepped forward as a whistleblower,
former Air Force special ops, combat
control, helicopter pilot. He told News
Nation he worked on secret UFO retrieval
missions for 30 years. Barbara described
flying a helicopter to recovery sites,
lifting objects from the ground. One
night, he got within 150 ft of something
he'd never seen before. An egg-shaped
craft, white, metallic, about the size
of an SUV. No engine, no thermal
signature. News Nation verified his
credentials. Three other military vets
backed up his story on camera. Russ
Coldart, the journalist covering it,
said the footage was about to change
everything. But he said that before.
This is why I'm skeptical of UFO
whistleblowers. Their stories always
have high SCOP scores. So, let's score
Jake Barber.
Timing five out of five. The story broke
in Ja

claims.rationale: Barber's January 2025 News Nation appearance, his Skywatcher Inc. company, and the upcoming book are real and publicly reported; that the rollout used classic media-launch tactics is straightforwardly true. Whether his underlying claims about UFO retrieval are true is unknowable. The host's skeptical framing of the rollout is reasonable; I keep directTruth moderate because 'this is a SCOP' is interpretive.

claims.text: A high NCI score by itself proves only that information is being delivered with manipulative tactics — not that the underlying claim is false; UFO disclosure could plausibly be (a) real disclosure being packaged, (b) a cover story for classified aircraft, or (c) a distraction from the more newsworthy military drone sightings.

evidence.quote:
three things. One, disclosure is real,
but it's being packaged, meaning the
government is revealing the truth, but
using SCAP techniques to control how,
when, and what we're allowed to know.
Option two, it's a full scop. Classified
aircraft testing needs a cover story.
UFOs are perfect. As long as we're
debating whether a craft is from Zeta
Reticuli or LISA, we're not asking
Lockheed or Rathon what they're up to.
Option three, it's a distraction.
Remember all the drones flying over the
bases for weeks? That was the real
story. But a UFO story pops up and
captures attention. And notice how the
drone story just vaporized.
Since 2017, this disclosure narrative
has been building. David Grush, Lu
Alzando, congressional hearings, leaked
videos. Each story scores high for
manipulation tactics. But each could
still contain some truth. And that's the
gray area

claims.rationale: This is the host's three-option taxonomy for interpreting UFO disclosure. The taxonomy itself is a reasonable speculative framework — each option has historical precedent (e.g., U-2/Aurora cover stories really did use UFO confusion; news cycles really do displace each other). I omit directTruth because this is explicitly speculative branching, not a verifiable claim.

dependencies.rationale: Generalizes the Barber-specific case into how to interpret UFO disclosure overall

claims.text: The strategic purpose of modern partisan-political SCOPs is to keep ordinary Americans fighting each other so that we stop scrutinizing the powerful — meaning every emotional political fight on Facebook or X is itself an act of being manipulated.

>> Yeah. What you talking about, human?
>> Think of it this way. Two neighbors. One
is a stuffy conservative. The other is a
bleeding heart liberal. Now, those

claims.rationale: The descriptive observation that engagement-driven platforms reward outrage and fragment political coalitions is well supported (Haugen revelations, multiple academic studies). The stronger framing — that this is a deliberate strategy by 'the real power' to suppress scrutiny — is the host's interpretation, plausible but not provable. Hence directTruth in the middle.

claims.text: Once you internalize the NCI pattern you will see manipulation everywhere — in news, expert claims, and corporate messaging — and that universal suspicion is itself the meta-SCOP outcome that powerful actors want, because it produces paralysis.

claims.rationale: This is a tension-resolving editorial: the host advocates the tool while warning it can drive cynicism. The empirical claim that universal suspicion produces paralysis is sociologically defensible (literature on epistemic learned helplessness) but the 'meta-SCOP' framing is explicitly the host's interpretation, so I omit directTruth.
